hero.subtitle: E-Farm is an autonomous robot that detects, localises and gently picks ripe fruit in greenhouse rows — pairing a YOLOv8 vision model with LiDAR depth and a millimetre-precision 6-axis arm.
hero.imageAlt: Rendering of the E-Farm autonomous harvesting robot in a greenhouse row

problem.title: Greenhouses are running out of hands
problem.lead: Protected cropping is expanding while the agricultural workforce shrinks. Harvest labour is seasonal, scarce and increasingly expensive — and ripe fruit will not wait.

stats.ftaLabel: Full-time farm jobs lost in the EU, 2009–2024
stats.youngLabel: Of EU farms run by people under 40
stats.usLabel: Open agricultural jobs in the US (56% of farmers report shortages)

problem.note: Greenhouse area worldwide is large and growing: Netherlands ~10,260 ha, Spain ~76,600 ha (Almería ~40,000 ha). The structured, repeatable rows of protected cropping are an ideal environment for selective robotic harvesting.

how.title: A four-stage harvesting pipeline
how.lead: Each ripe fruit flows through the same closed loop — perception to manipulation — many times per minute.

steps.detectBody: A YOLOv8 model classifies fruit and ripeness in the live camera feed, isolating only the produce ready to pick.
steps.localizeBody: LiDAR and RGB-D depth fuse with the detection to place each fruit in 3D space with sub-centimetre accuracy.
steps.graspBody: The PAROL6 6-axis arm plans a collision-aware path and closes a force-controlled gripper for a gentle, bruise-free pick.
steps.depositBody: Harvested fruit is placed into the onboard tray; telemetry and yield data stream to the fleet dashboard.

tech.title: Built on open robotics foundations
tech.lead: E-Farm composes proven open-source building blocks into a production harvesting stack — auditable, extensible and credibly engineered.

cards.visionBody: YOLOv8 (Ultralytics) detection and ripeness classification, trained via Roboflow on 10,000+ labeled images.
cards.lidarBody: LiDAR + Intel RealSense RGB-D fusion for robust 3D fruit localisation under variable greenhouse lighting.
cards.armBody: PAROL6 open-hardware manipulator (GPLv3) driven through MoveIt 2 motion planning.
cards.rosBody: ROS 2 Humble orchestrates perception, planning and control as composable, testable nodes.
cards.bmsBody: LiFePO4 pack with an ESP32-based BMS, informed by the foxBMS open BMS reference.
cards.fleetBody: MQTT telemetry feeds a web fleet dashboard and a companion mobile app for remote monitoring.

tech.foundations: Open-source foundations: Ultralytics YOLOv8 · PAROL6 · MoveIt 2 · Intel RealSense · foxBMS · ROS 2.

specs.title: Technical specifications
specs.lead: Engineering figures for the current prototype. Items marked “target” are design goals for the pilot and production builds.

ai.title: Seeing ripeness, not just fruit
ai.lead: E-Farm’s own YOLOv8 model reaches 98% ripeness-detection accuracy on its project test set, trained on 10,000+ labeled images spanning five crops.
ai.claimBody: 98% ripeness-detection accuracy on the project test set (YOLOv8 trained via Roboflow on 10,000+ images, 5 crops). This is our internal evaluation, reported as claimed.
ai.contextBody: For external comparison, published and commercial systems report ripeness/selection accuracies in a similar range — e.g. Four Growers cite 98% ripe-selection, tomato studies report ~86%, and strawberry studies ~95%. These are independent results, distinct from E-Farm’s own claim.
ai.mockCaption: Illustrative detection overlay — bounding boxes and confidence are for demonstration.

bms.title: Energy that lasts a shift
bms.lead: A LiFePO4 battery pack supervised by an ESP32-based battery-management system delivers a targeted ~8 hours of runtime, with live cell telemetry for safety and uptime.
bms.point1: LiFePO4 chemistry for long cycle life and thermal stability.
bms.point2: ESP32 BMS measures per-cell voltage, current and temperature.
bms.point3: Telemetry streams to the fleet dashboard for predictive maintenance.
bms.attribution: BMS architecture informed by the foxBMS open-source reference.

chassis.title: A base built for the rows
chassis.lead: E-Farm’s mobile base derives from the NASA JPL Open Source Rover’s rocker-differential geometry, keeping all wheels grounded on uneven greenhouse floors and ramps.
chassis.point1: Rocker-differential suspension for stable traversal of bumps and rails.
chassis.point2: Compact footprint sized to standard greenhouse row spacing.
chassis.point3: Low centre of gravity carrying battery, compute and arm.
chassis.diagramAlt: Schematic side view of the rocker-differential chassis

market.title: A market growing toward $56B
market.lead: The agricultural-robots market is projected to grow from USD 17.73B in 2025 to USD 56.26B by 2030 — a 26.0% CAGR. Selective harvesting is one of its hardest, highest-value frontiers.
market.chartSource: Source: MarketsandMarkets, 2025. Yearly values interpolated between the 2025 and 2030 anchors.

competitors.title: Where E-Farm fits
competitors.lead: The harvesting-robot field is real and well-funded. E-Farm’s differentiator is an open, auditable stack covering five greenhouse crops at a target price built for emerging-market growers. Click any column to sort.
competitors.sortHint: Click a column header to sort.

refs.title: Selected references
refs.lead: Peer-reviewed and preprint literature that grounds E-Farm’s perception, localisation and harvesting approach.

team.title: Three years of focused R&D
team.lead: E-Farm was founded in 2022 at Gebze Bilişim Vadisi (Informatics Valley). A five-person multidisciplinary team has spent three years turning a research idea into a working prototype.
team.note: We are an engineering team, not a marketing one — every claim on this page is something we can demonstrate or cite.

roadmap.title: From idea to fleet
roadmap.lead: Where we have been and where we are going. Milestones from 2025 onward are targets, not completed work.

tiers.title: Back the harvest
tiers.lead: Choose a pledge tier. All prices are illustrative targets for the campaign and may change before the term sheet is finalised.
tiers.termSheet: Pledges are commitments to the campaign, not a final purchase. Final pricing, specifications and delivery terms will be set out in the backer term sheet before any charge.

resources.title: Backer resources
resources.lead: Documentation for evaluators, partners and backers. Items marked “ready” are available now; others publish during the campaign.

faq.title: Questions, answered honestly
faq.lead: The things backers and growers ask most. If something is uncertain, we say so.

items.cropsA: The vision model is trained for five crops: strawberry, tomato, pepper, cucumber and eggplant. Performance varies by crop and growing system; strawberry and tomato are the most mature today.
items.accuracyA: It is the ripeness-detection accuracy of our YOLOv8 model on our own held-out project test set — an internal evaluation, reported as claimed. It is not a guarantee of pick success in every greenhouse; field performance depends on lighting, occlusion and crop layout.
items.deliveryQ: When will robots ship, and what are the risks?
items.deliveryA: E-Farm is a prototype moving toward pilot. The roadmap targets pre-series production in 2026 and fleet rollout in 2027. Hardware timelines carry real risk: supply chains, certification and field validation can slip. We will communicate changes transparently.
items.opensourceQ: Why build on PAROL6 and other open-source projects?
items.opensourceA: Open foundations like PAROL6 (GPLv3), MoveIt 2 and ROS 2 are auditable, well-documented and avoid vendor lock-in. They let a small team build credibly and let backers inspect what they are funding. We honour each project’s licence.
items.safetyQ: Is it safe, and what about CE marking?
items.safetyA: The robot operates in structured greenhouse rows with force-controlled grasping. Full machinery-safety compliance and CE marking are part of the pilot-to-production path and are not yet complete; we treat them as gating requirements before commercial delivery.
items.refundQ: What is the refund / cancellation policy?
items.refundA: Pledges are campaign commitments, not final purchases. Refund and cancellation terms will be defined in the backer term sheet before any charge is finalised. Reserve now to lock a slot without obligation to complete.
items.privacyA: Reservation form data is sent only to the E-Farm team to follow up with you, via Formspree. We do not sell your data. Robot telemetry stays within the grower’s fleet account.
items.reservationQ: How does the grower reservation work?
items.reservationA: The reservation form registers your interest as a commercial grower — crop, greenhouse size and contact. It is non-binding and simply puts you in the pilot queue and on our priority-deployment list.

form.title: Reserve as a grower
form.lead: Tell us about your greenhouse and we’ll keep you in the pilot queue. No payment, no obligation.
form.messagePlaceholder: Anything you’d like us to know about your operation…

form.error: Something went wrong. Please try again, or email us directly.
form.note: Submissions are delivered to the E-Farm team. We never sell your data.
